lesson.menu_desc: Writing your first professional email in English to a colleague in Rwanda -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 4 -- Professional Email: Subject, Greeting, Body, Sign-off
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Carlos Eduardo | Aula 4 -- Professional Email

vocab.title: Match each email word to its meaning
defs: the title line of the email; what it is about
to add a file to an email
to say that something is correct or received
to send an email you received to another person
the last day or time to finish something
comments to help you improve your work
a polite word to end an email (Best regards)

reading.rtitle: A Message to the Rwanda Office
paras: Subject: Updated farm checklist for review. Hi Jean, I hope this email finds you well. I am Carlos, the sustainability analyst in Brazil. I attach the updated farm checklist for the southern Minas Gerais suppliers. Please review it and send me your feedback.
The deadline for the report is next Friday. Could you confirm that you receive the file? If you have questions, please reply to this email. I look forward to your reply. Best regards, Carlos.

A professional email has four parts. First, the subject line says what the email is about. Second, the greeting starts the message: Hi Jean, or Dear Jean. Third, the body has the message: I attach..., please review..., could you confirm...? Fourth, the sign-off ends the email: Best regards, and your name. To a colleague, Hi is fine. To the Swiss headquarters, Dear is more formal.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- A1 reading, a model professional email

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Carlos writes a coffee recipe for the Rwanda team.
Carlos sends a farm checklist by email and asks for feedback, and the text shows the four parts of a professional email.
The text explains how to grow coffee in Rwanda.

items: Carlos attaches the updated farm checklist.
The text says: 'I attach the updated farm checklist.'
The deadline for the report is next Monday.
The text says: 'The deadline for the report is next Friday.'
Carlos asks Jean to confirm that he receives the file.
The text says: 'Could you confirm that you receive the file?'
A professional email has two parts.
The text says a professional email has FOUR parts: subject, greeting, body, and sign-off.

parts: that you receive the file? Best

gapfill.text: Remember the four parts: subject (what it is about), greeting (Hi Jean,), body (I attach... please... could you...?), and sign-off (Best regards,). Use a comma after the greeting and after 'Best regards'.

bank.label: Useful sentences for a professional email
items: I hope this email finds you well.
I attach the updated farm checklist.
Could you confirm that you receive the file?
I look forward to your reply.

lf.title: Which part of the email is it?
items: the checklist and ask for feedback.

lf.text: Subject = what it is about. Greeting = Hi Jean, / Dear Jean,. Body = your message. Sign-off = Best regards, + your name.

items.situation: Write a subject line for an email about a farm checklist.
Start the email to Jean, your colleague in Rwanda.
Say that you add the file to the email.
tips: I attach the updated farm checklist.

items.situation: Ask Jean to reply and confirm he receives it. Then end the email.
tips: Could you confirm that you receive the file?
I look forward to your reply. Best regards, Carlos.

items: Say the four parts of your email out loud: subject, greeting, one body sentence, and the sign-off. Use your notes.
Say the email again, and add one request: 'Could you confirm that you receive the file?'
Now say the whole email with no notes, from subject to sign-off. Sound natural, not memorized.
Jean replies: 'I do not have the file.' Answer politely: 'Sorry, I attach it again. Please confirm.'

answerkey.title: Reveal the model email
list: Subject: Updated farm checklist for review

I hope this email finds you well. I attach the updated farm checklist for the southern Minas Gerais suppliers. Please review it and send me your feedback. The deadline is next Friday. Could you confirm that you receive the file?
I look forward to your reply. Best regards, Carlos.
answerkey.note: Four parts: subject, greeting (with a comma), body (the message), and sign-off (Best regards, + your name).

listenings.text: Hi Carlos, this is Emma. Let me read you a simple model email, part by part. First, the subject: Updated farm checklist for review. Then the greeting: Hi Jean. Then the body: I hope this email finds you well. I attach the updated farm checklist. Please review it and send me your feedback. The deadline is next Friday. Could you confirm that you receive the file? Finally, the sign-off: I look forward to your reply. Best regards, Carlos. That is all. A subject, a greeting, a body, and a sign-off.

Hi Carlos, a quick note about writing emails at Sucafina. To a colleague like Jean in Rwanda, Hi is fine, and you can be direct. To the Swiss headquarters, Dear is more formal, and you write with more care. Always add a clear subject line, so the reader knows the topic. Always attach the file before you send. And always end with Best regards and your name. Keep it short and polite. Good luck with your first email.

extra_audio.text: First, Carlos writes the subject line. Then he writes the greeting: Hi Jean. Then he writes the body and attaches the checklist. He asks Jean to confirm. Finally, he ends with Best regards and sends the email.
